title: Altitude
description: A Foddian climbing game where you swing two pickaxes to scale a massive map.

content.text: Pre Production
Note
Altitude was made at the start of 2025 — I'm writing this in 2026, working from my original notes. Three people worked on it, myself included, over the span of one month.
Description
Altitude was heavily inspired by "A Difficult Game About Climbing": armed with two pickaxes, you climb a large map and try to reach the top.
Mechanics
Left Click Down: Swing the left pickaxe.
Right Click Down: Swing the right pickaxe.
Release: Lift the corresponding pickaxe back out of its swing.
While a mouse button is held, the pickaxe stays swung and moving the mouse swings your body from it. With neither button held, both pickaxes are up and you fall.

The game shipped with narration, sound effects, and complete main, settings, and pause menus. The level and win state were finished. There's no explicit lose state — falling is the lose state. It's a Foddian game.
My Contribution
I was the 3D modeler, level designer, scripter, and menu designer.
Post Production
Game Links
Play It Here: [Altitude on itch.io](https://scin.itch.io/altitude)
Problems & Failures

The headline issue was the ragdoll. With a pickaxe lifted, a player with high mouse sensitivity could whip the mouse and launch themselves into the void — or, just as easily, straight to the end of the game.

The other honest problem: it looked rough. The character model had no texture, hair, or detail; there was no post-processing, so everything read flat and dull. I'd only been using Blender for about a year at that point. My attempt at a parallax background like the one in our inspiration didn't land, and the water was a flat plane.
And the game leaned too close to its inspiration — it ended up a rougher version of the original rather than its own game.
Fixes & Improvements

Remaking this, I'd replace the ragdoll with proper IK for the arms. I tried at the time — I must have watched Pontypants' devlog twenty times — but IK was beyond me then. It isn't now. I'd keep ragdoll physics on the legs, where the looseness reads as natural rather than chaotic.

The visuals would get a full overhaul: volumetric fog, detailed models in place of flat colors, a proper water shader, and a real parallax background — a technique I haven't built yet, but one that's well documented and very learnable.
Some models also shipped without lighting — something I only noticed reviewing the project later. That gets fixed too.
Personal Reflection

This project was a trial by fire. I took a short weekend vacation right before the deadline and came back to the worst level design I have ever seen — so I rebuilt the entire level myself. The main menu, my responsibility, hadn't been started either, which was bad planning on my part. In the final day I finished the level, the win menu, the main menu, and the missing sound effects. To this day I can't swear the level is 100% beatable — I believe it is; the only truly hard jumps are at the start where I could test thoroughly — but given that nobody is speedrunning Altitude, I've made my peace with it.
